title: Cat Diarrhea Outside the Litter Box: Causes and Triage Guide
meta_desc: Find out why your cat is having diarrhea outside the box. Learn about urgency levels and how a photo can help your vet provide better care.
content_html:
TL;DR: Diarrhea outside the litter box is typically a medical issue caused by physical urgency and requires a veterinary consultation within 24 hours, or immediate emergency care if accompanied by vomiting or lethargy.

Why is my cat having diarrhea outside of the litter box?

  • Diarrhea is characterized by loose, watery, or unformed stools.
  • Defecating outside the box is often due to gastrointestinal "urgency," meaning the cat cannot reach their box in time.
  • This is usually a physical health issue related to inflammation, infection, or dietary indiscretion rather than a behavioral problem.

When is my cat's diarrhea considered a medical emergency?

  • Urgency Level: Medium. While a single bout may not be life-threatening, persistent diarrhea can quickly lead to dehydration.
  • You should contact a veterinarian within 24 hours for most cases.
  • Seek immediate emergency care if the diarrhea is accompanied by vomiting, extreme lethargy, or pale gums.
  • Seek emergency care immediately if the stool appears black and tarry.

How does taking a photo of my cat's stool help with the triage process?

  • A clear photo allows the veterinary team to evaluate the color, consistency, and any presence of blood or mucus.
  • This visual evidence helps the vet differentiate between small-bowel and large-bowel issues.
  • A photo helps determine the severity of the clinical signs and how quickly your pet needs to be seen.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Feline coronavirus (FCoV) infection can cause transient, mild diarrhea and/or vomiting due to viral replication in enterocytes. Infected kittens may exhibit stunted growth or upper respiratory signs. Occasionally, severe diarrhea with weight loss, unresponsive to treatment, may persist for months. Feline infectious peritonitis (FIP) should be considered in cats with non-specific signs such as chronic weight loss or fever of unknown origin resistant to antibiotics or recurrent in nature. Clinical signs of FIP vary depending on organ involvement due to vasculitis.

Chapter: Gastroenterology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 281)

faqs.question: Why did my cat stop using the box for diarrhea?
faqs.answer: Intestinal inflammation creates a sudden urge to go. Your cat likely tried to reach the box but couldn't make it in time due to the intensity of the cramping.

faqs.question: Can I give my cat human anti-diarrheal medicine?
faqs.answer: No. Never give human medications to cats without a specific veterinary prescription, as many common over-the-counter drugs are toxic to felines.

faqs.question: What are common causes of sudden diarrhea?
faqs.answer: Common causes include sudden diet changes, intestinal parasites, bacterial infections, or ingesting something toxic or indigestible.
